mcp_meta_description: Documentation for the MCP integration (Model Context Protocol) in WSJF Planning. Connect VS Code, Claude Code or Copilot CLI with your PI planning data.
mcp_meta_og_description: Connect your AI tools directly with your SAFe PI planning data via the Model Context Protocol.
mcp_hero_description: Connect your AI tools directly with your SAFe PI planning data. Access projects, features, teams and skills from VS Code, Claude Code or Copilot CLI.
mcp_what_is_text: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that enables AI applications to securely access external data sources and tools. WSJF Planning provides an MCP server that allows your AI tools to read your PI planning data — all within your tenant context and with your permissions.
mcp_auth_text: The MCP server uses API Tokens (Bearer Authentication) for identification. Each token is linked to your user and tenant — all data is automatically scoped to your tenant.
mcp_auth_step3: Give a descriptive name (e.g. 'VS Code', 'Claude Code')
mcp_auth_step4: Copy the token — it's only shown once!
mcp_auth_warning: ⚠️ Treat your API token like a password. Do not share it or store it in public repositories.
mcp_vscode_text: VS Code natively supports MCP servers via settings.json or a .vscode/mcp.json file in the project directory.
mcp_vscode_hint: Replace YOUR_API_TOKEN with your personal token from the Settings. After saving, VS Code automatically detects the MCP server and the tools become available in the Copilot Chat.
mcp_claude_text: Claude Code (Anthropic CLI) supports MCP servers via a JSON configuration. Create or update the file ~/.claude/claude_desktop_config.json.
mcp_claude_hint: After restarting Claude Code, the WSJF Planning tools are available in context. You can then ask, for example: 'Show me all features in project X sorted by WSJF score.'
mcp_copilot_cli_text: The GitHub Copilot CLI agent also supports MCP servers. Update the file ~/.copilot/mcp-config.json (user-level configuration) or use the /mcp add command inside the CLI.
mcp_copilot_cli_hint: Important: Use type "sse" instead of "http". With type "http", the CLI attempts OAuth authentication which fails. With "sse", the Bearer token is used directly. Also ensure the top-level key is "mcpServers" (not "servers") — the CLI will fail to start otherwise.
mcp_test_text: You can test the connection beforehand with curl:
mcp_test_success: A successful response contains a JSON-RPC result with server capabilities and the tool list.
mcp_tools_resource_text: Additionally, the resource dashboard-summary is available, which provides an overview of your tenant data (project, feature, team, skill and planning counts).
mcp_security_tenant: Tenant Isolation: All queries are limited to your tenant. You only see your organization's data.
mcp_security_rw: Read & Write: The MCP server provides read access to all entities and write access to features (create, update, delete). Other entities are currently read-only.
mcp_security_token: Token-based: Each token can be revoked at any time in Settings. Tokens are tied to your user.
mcp_security_https: HTTPS: All communication is encrypted via HTTPS.
mcp_tool_list_projects: List all projects with project leaders and team count
mcp_tool_get_project: Detail information about a project including teams, skills, description
mcp_tool_create_project: Create a new project with project number, name, start date, and leader
mcp_tool_update_project: Update existing project — supports partial updates and status transitions
mcp_tool_list_features: Filter features by project, status or type — sorted by WSJF score
mcp_tool_get_feature: Feature details including WSJF scores, dependencies, skill requirements
mcp_tool_create_feature: Create a new feature with name, project, type, description etc.
mcp_tool_update_feature: Update existing feature — only provided fields are changed
mcp_tool_delete_feature: Delete feature (soft delete, can be undone)
mcp_tool_get_team: Team details with members and their skills
mcp_tool_list_plannings: PI plannings with project, owner, feature/iteration count
mcp_tool_get_feature_specification: Get the full specification of a feature (markdown content)
mcp_tool_create_feature_specification: Generate an AI-powered specification from the feature description
mcp_tool_update_feature_specification: Update the specification content with version tracking
mcp_tool_list_feature_plans: List all plan components of a feature with priority, dependencies and estimation
mcp_tool_create_feature_plan: Create plan components — manually or AI-generated from specification
mcp_tool_update_feature_plan: Update a plan's title, description, or status
mcp_tool_get_next_plan_step: Get the next actionable plan step (highest priority, all dependencies resolved)
feat_meta_description: Documentation for Spec-Driven Development in WSJF Planning. Learn about AI-powered specifications, plan generation, and the spec-kit workflow.
feat_hero_description: From feature idea to executable plan — powered by AI and aligned with GitHub's spec-kit methodology.
feat_workflow_step1_text: Start by creating a feature with a detailed description. This serves as the foundation for all subsequent artifacts.
feat_workflow_step2_text: Generate a structured specification from your description using AI. The spec follows GitHub's spec-kit format with scenarios, requirements, and success criteria.
feat_workflow_step3_text: Transform the specification into 3–7 actionable plan components with priorities (P1–P3), dependencies, and story point estimates.
feat_workflow_step4_text: Work through plan steps in dependency order. Use the MCP tool get-next-plan-step to pull the next actionable item.
feat_spec_ai_text: Specifications are generated from your feature description using AI, aligned with the spec-kit template format. They include user scenarios (Given/When/Then), numbered requirements (FR-xxx), key entities, and success criteria (SC-xxx).
feat_spec_edit_text: Edit specifications with an inline markdown editor. Use the AI Chat assistant in a side-by-side view to discuss and refine the document — the full text remains visible while you chat.
feat_spec_version_text: Every change creates a version snapshot. Browse the version history and compare any two versions with the built-in diff viewer.
feat_spec_reset_text: Need a fresh start? Regenerate the specification from the current feature description. The previous version is preserved in the history.
feat_plan_priority_text: Each plan component has a priority: P1 (Must Have), P2 (Should Have), or P3 (Nice to Have). Plans are color-coded for quick identification.
feat_plan_deps_text: Plan steps can depend on other steps. Dependencies are tracked and visualized — you can see which prerequisites are resolved (✓) or still pending (○).
feat_plan_status_text: Plans follow a simple workflow: Open → Implemented or Obsolete. When regenerating plans, existing open plans are marked as obsolete while implemented plans are preserved.
feat_plan_estimation_text: Each plan component includes a PERT-style estimate with best case, most likely, and worst case values. The weighted average is calculated automatically.
feat_mcp_text: All artifacts are accessible via MCP tools. Use get-next-plan-step to pull the next actionable plan component in your AI-powered development workflow.
feat_speckit_text: WSJF Planning follows the GitHub spec-kit template structure. Specifications use structured scenarios with Given/When/Then format, numbered requirements (FR-xxx), and success criteria (SC-xxx). Plans are decomposed into user stories with goals, independent tests, acceptance scenarios, and task checklists.
feat_meta_description: Documentation for the feature workflow in WSJF Planning. Learn how spec-driven development turns feature descriptions into executable plans.
feat_meta_og_description: Spec-driven development: from feature description to AI-generated specifications and executable plans.
feat_hero_description: WSJF Planning follows a spec-kit methodology: describe a feature, generate a specification, derive a plan, and execute — all AI-assisted and fully traceable.
feat_wf_description: Every feature follows a four-step pipeline from idea to implementation. Each step builds on the previous one and is supported by AI assistance.
feat_wf_step1_text: Write a concise description of the feature you want to build. This is the starting point for everything that follows.
feat_wf_step2_text: An AI generates a structured specification from your description, aligned with the GitHub spec-kit template format.
feat_wf_step3_text: From the specification, the AI derives an executable plan with 3–7 prioritized components, dependencies, and estimates.
feat_wf_step4_text: Work through plan steps one by one. Track status, mark completion, and use MCP tools for AI-assisted implementation.
feat_spec_description: Specifications are the heart of the workflow. They translate a free-text feature description into a structured, reviewable document that drives planning.
feat_spec_ai_text: Specifications are generated from the feature description using AI, aligned with GitHub spec-kit templates including scenarios, requirements, and tasks.
feat_spec_editor_text: Edit the generated specification directly with a built-in markdown editor. Full markdown support with live preview.
feat_spec_chat_text: Refine the specification collaboratively using the AI chat assistant in a side-by-side view. Ask questions, request changes, or explore alternatives.
feat_spec_versions_text: Every change is versioned. Browse previous versions and compare them with a built-in diff viewer to track how the specification evolved.
feat_spec_reset_text: Not happy with the result? Reset the specification and regenerate it from the feature description at any time.
feat_plan_description: Plans break a specification down into actionable, prioritized components. Each plan is AI-generated and fully editable.
feat_plan_ai_text: The AI generates 3–7 plan components from the specification. Each component represents a concrete, implementable work item.
feat_plan_priority_text: Each component has a priority level: P1 (Must Have), P2 (Should Have), or P3 (Nice to Have). This drives implementation order.
feat_plan_deps_text: Define dependencies between plan steps. The system tracks which components must be completed before others can begin.
feat_plan_status_text: Each plan component follows a status workflow: Open → Implemented or Obsolete. Track progress across the entire plan.
feat_plan_estimation_text: Estimate effort with best case, likely case, and worst case values. The system calculates a weighted average (PERT) automatically.
feat_mcp_text: Plan steps integrate with the Model Context Protocol. AI coding agents can use the get-next-plan-step tool to retrieve the next actionable task and update its status upon completion.
feat_mcp_hint: The MCP server exposes plan-related tools so AI agents can work through plans autonomously.
feat_speckit_description: Specifications follow the GitHub spec-kit format, a proven template for structured feature documentation used across many open-source projects.
feat_speckit_scenarios_text: Behavioral scenarios describe expected system behavior in a structured format. Each scenario has preconditions (Given), actions (When), and expected outcomes (Then).
feat_speckit_requirements_text: Requirements are labeled with unique identifiers (FR-001, FR-002, …) for traceability. Each maps to specific plan components.
feat_speckit_tasks_text: Implementation tasks use a numbered format (T001, T002, …) with clear descriptions, acceptance criteria, and estimated effort.
